coredata.dc:title: Unravelling the unique roles of NCQDs over CeFeO3 perovskite as highly efficient photoluminescent solar induced peroxymonosulfate/photocatalyst system: Optimization of removal process, mechanism and degradation pathway
coredata.dc:creator: Tuna, Özlem
Bilgin Simsek, Esra
coredata.dc:description: In this work, novel ferrite type CeFeO3 perovskite was elaborately designed with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) for effective degradation of ciprofloxacin (CPX) under peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activated photocatalytic system. The critic roles of NCQDs addition in the PMS-activated system were examined through characterization and photocatalytic tests. The as-fabricated CeFeO3 @NCQDs heterojunction showed superior catalytic performance (92.5 %) which was 2.8 times higher than that of bare CeFeO3 (32.0 %). It was found through XPS analysis that new oxygen-rich bonds were formed in the CeFeO3 @NCQDs sample, providing new reaction sites. PL technique showed that the carrier separation efficiency of the composite was significantly superior to that of the sole CeFeO3 due to excellent electron transfer property of NCQDs. Furthermore, the change in the band energy potentials improved the oxidation ability, resulting in increased degradation rate. The heterojunction catalyst also showed high removal efficiency in real water matrices. Meanwhile, radical trapping tests showed that the superoxide radicals play dominant roles in the photodegradation system. The as-synthesized catalyst also exhibited satisfactory performance in dye decomposition reactions. In addition, Box–Behnken design was applied to find out the optimum conditions which were determined as catalyst dosage of 0.80 g/L, PMS concentration of 4.90 mM and initial CPX concentration of 7.00 mg/L. The CeFeO3 @NCQDs/Vis/PMS system could be considered as a promising candidate in the aspect of practical wastewater treatment applications.

head.ce:title: Unravelling the unique roles of NCQDs over CeFeO3 perovskite as highly efficient photoluminescent solar induced peroxymonosulfate/photocatalyst system: Optimization of removal process, mechanism and degradation pathway

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Yalova University, 77100 Yalova, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Yalova University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Yalova University, 77100 Yalova, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, novel ferrite type CeFeO3 perovskite was elaborately designed with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) for effective degradation of ciprofloxacin (CPX) under peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activated photocatalytic system. The critic roles of NCQDs addition in the PMS-activated system were examined through characterization and photocatalytic tests. The as-fabricated CeFeO3 @NCQDs heterojunction showed superior catalytic performance (92.5 %) which was 2.8 times higher than that of bare CeFeO3 (32.0 %). It was found through XPS analysis that new oxygen-rich bonds were formed in the CeFeO3 @NCQDs sample, providing new reaction sites. PL technique showed that the carrier separation efficiency of the composite was significantly superior to that of the sole CeFeO3 due to excellent electron transfer property of NCQDs. Furthermore, the change in the band energy potentials improved the oxidation ability, resulting in increased degradation rate. The heterojunction catalyst also showed high removal efficiency in real water matrices. Meanwhile, radical trapping tests showed that the superoxide radicals play dominant roles in the photodegradation system. The as-synthesized catalyst also exhibited satisfactory performance in dye decomposition reactions. In addition, Box–Behnken design was applied to find out the optimum conditions which were determined as catalyst dosage of 0.80 g/L, PMS concentration of 4.90 mM and initial CPX concentration of 7.00 mg/L. The CeFeO3 @NCQDs/Vis/PMS system could be considered as a promising candidate in the aspect of practical wastewater treatment applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic remediation technologies have attracted special attention to degrade emerging and recalcitrant pollutants, owing to their high efficiency, environmentally friendly and cost-effectiveness features. Perovskite based materials have been presented as highly potential photocatalysts since they can capture a wide range of solar energy which enhances catalytic reaction. Perovskite oxides are complex metal oxides with the general formula of ABO3 and according to different lattice distortion of A- and B-sites, they are mainly divided into three groups, namely lanthanide (LaBO3), titanate (ATiO3) and ferrite (AFeO3) materials [1]. Of these, ferrite-type perovskites have shown excellent performance towards many organic contaminants, on account of their unique crystal structure and electronic properties [2]. The crystal structure offers a good framework for tuning band gap energies to enable extended light absorption while the electronic features provide short exciton diffusion length and low conduction band potential, resulting sufficient separation of photogenerated species [3]. Among the ferrite-type perovskites, LaFeO3, BiFeO3 and SrFeO3 catalysts have been well examined by researchers since they have proven to exhibit extraordinary performance [4]. Nowadays, cerium ferrite (CeFeO3) perovskite has been newly explored as a promising semiconductor owing to its abundant oxygen vacancies and high reduction ability providing high decomposition potential. For instance, Anantharaman et al. [5] showed that Mg-incorporated into CeFeO3 could be utilized as a photo-fenton catalyst for dye degradation. In other study, Cu-species were coupled with the ferrite structure to enhance decomposition of dye molecules [6]. However, pristine perovskites have inherent drawbacks such as low surface area and rapid recombination of photoinduced electro-hole pairs which are unfavorable for efficient radical generation [2]. In this view, modification strategies such as heterojunction with graphitic carbon nitride [7] or supporting on larger matrix [8] have been applied to improve the degradation efficiency of CeFeO3 in the field of photocatalysis.

Carbon dots (CDs) or carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel class of zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with high quantum yield, have attracted considerable attention due to their fascinating features such as chemical inertness, excellent stability, flexibility in surface functionalization, easiness of preparation, and biocompatibility [9]. Moreover, due to the unique optical and electronic properties, CQDs promote the rate of electron transfer, increase the amount of the active electrons, and enhance visible light absorption property when they were coupled with perovskite-type semiconductors [10]. Li et. al [11] successfully deposited CQDs on SmFeO3 structure through sol-gel method followed by impregnation and the hybrid sample was utilized in NO oxidation under sun-light in a continuous-flow system. It was observed that CQDs significantly enhanced light absorption ability, resulting 90 % NO removal through Z-scheme heterojunction mechanism. Paszkiewicz-Gawron et al. [12] examined the photocatalytic performance of SrTiO3, SrSnO3 and AgTaO3 coupled with CQDs for phenol decomposition and the addition of CQDs greatly promoted the activity of pristine perovskites. On the other hand, the effect of CQDs was compared with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and it was observed that the transfer of the photogenerated electrons was more favorable in the presence of GQDs due to their well-crystallized structure. In this point, it can be stated that the electronic properties of CQDs should be improved to observe highly efficient decomposition rate. For that, recent approach has been presented as doping CQDs with nitrogen atoms which have strong electron affinity to increase the lifetime of the photoactive charges and thereby decreasing working function of CQDs, leading to higher photocatalytic activity [13]. Hence, we examined the introduction of NCQDs into CeFeO3 perovskite structure to facilitate the degradation of recalcitrant organic contaminants. To the best of our knowledge, there is no investigation about construction of CeFeO3 with NCQDs as high-performance photocatalytic decomposition under visible light.

In current studies, peroxymonosulfate (PMS)- based advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have been widely utilized to oxidize pollutant into low toxicity of small molecules due to its superior decomposition ability in the presence of light irradiation and transition metal ions, especially ferrous species [14]. Visible light driven-persulfate/iron-based catalysts systems have been deemed to be the most promising water treatment processes since the occurrence of the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle in the presence of PMS contributes the generation of the sulfate and hydroxy radicals which are responsible for decomposition activity [15]. Furthermore, persulfate can stabilize photoinduced electrons, inhibiting the recombination rate that promotes photocatalytic reaction rate [16]. The advantageous behavior of Fe-based perovskite/Vis/PMS system was observed in the previous study: CoFe2O4, CuFe2O4, MnFe2O4 and ZnFe2O4 were shown to effectively catalyze PMS for the degradation of phthalate compounds and exhibited excellent activity and stability [17–19]. Furthermore, it was observed that LaFeO3 could be easily activated by PMS, facilitating the separation of the photogenerated species[20,21] However, the magnetic ferrites have been struggling with easy aggregation and recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [19]. To improve the catalytic performance, new approach has been presented as the modification of ferrite-type perovskite with carbon-based materials.

Herein, CeFeO3 perovskite catalyst decorated with NCQDs was fabricated by performing ultrasonication followed by mixing method. Initially, various analyses were carried out to determine morphological, structural, textural, and optical characteristics of the as-synthesized samples. Further studies were conducted to examine the performance of the photocatalysts in peroxymonosulfate (PMS)-activated ciprofloxacin (CPX) removal under visible light irradiation. The operating parameters (catalyst dosage, PMS concentration and initial CPX concentration) were optimized by applying Box-Behnken design in response surface methodology. Furthermore, the photocatalytic mechanism was investigated with the help of trapping experiments, also the transformation products and possible degradation pathway were investigated. In addition, to generalize the utilization of the above findings, the effect of different water matrixes and potential of reusability of the catalyst were also evaluated. Finally, the CeFeO3/NCQDs/Vis/PMS catalytic system was tested towards several dye molecules. This work will provide new perspectives into photocatalytic performance of CeFeO3 as a novel ferrite-type perovskite and its integration with nitrogen enriched carbon quantum dots for enhanced sulfate activated system.

Pristine CeFeO3 was fabricated via precipitation method. In this method, 4.342 g of Ce(NO3)30.6 H2O and 2.703 g of FeCl3•9 H2O (99 %) were dissolved in 100 mL distilled water. After their complete dissolution, NH4OH (28 %, v/v) was added to adjust the solution pH to 8 for the CeFeO3 precipitate. Thereafter, the mixture was rigorously stirred for 24 h to complete the precipitation. Then, the dark yellowish suspension was filtered, and the resultant solid was transferred to an alumina crucible and was heated in the muffle furnace at 600°C for 4 h in an air atmosphere.

The N-coupled with CQDs were prepared by a facile thermal method. Briefly, 1.26 g of melamine and 2.10 g of citric acid monohydrate were dissolved in 50 mL of distilled water, then the solution was rigorously stirred at 40 ºC for 2 h. In further step, the solution was treated in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at 210 ºC for 18 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resultant solution was filtered to remove larger particles. Finally, light-yellow suspension of N-CQDs was obtained.

For heterostructured samples, 30 mg of CeFeO3 particles were dispersed in NCQDs-included distilled water solution (20 mL) under ultrasonic treatment and subsequently the attained suspension was stirred for 48 h. Then, the resultant particles were obtained by performing vacuum filtration. According to the volume of NCQDs solution (5–15 mL), three different photocatalysts were synthesized and labelled as CeFeO3@NCQDs-5, CeFeO3@NCQDs-10 and CeFeO @NCQDs-15, respectively. The synthesis procedures were schematically presented in Fig. 1.

The crystalline property of the as-fabricated photocatalysts was characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. The morphology of the samples was monitored using a field-emission scan electron microscopy (SEM-EDAX: Bruker). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were also obtained. Raman analysis was conducted to explore the structural changes of the composite during the fabrication stage. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to identify the chemical compositions and electronic structures. The optical properties of the heterostructure photocatalyst were obtained by applying diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) and photoluminescence (PL) analyses. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area analysis of the samples was performed using N2 adsorption–desorption technique.

Photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin (CPX) in a glass reactor placed into a rectangleshaped cabinet equipped with four visible lamps (Osram 150 W, λ: 400–800 nm) and two fans. Primarily, the reaction suspension was stirred in dark to ensure homogeneous mixing to reach adsorption/desorption equilibrium. During the photocatalytic experiments, 3 mL of each sample were collected at time intervals from 30 to 120 min and were filtered using a 0.45-µm-pore syringe filter. The residual antibiotic concentrations were analyzed by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy at wavelength of 278 nm.

Scavenger tests were conducted to identify the main radical oxidative species (ROS) in the CPX degradation. For this, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na, 1.0 mM), isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 1.0 mM), ethanol (EtOH, 1 mM) and p-benzoquinone (BQ, 1.0 mM) were added separately into the antibiotic solution. Furthermore, the effect of reaction environment was evaluated by carrying out the decomposition in drinking water, tap water, sea water and lake water-spiked CPX. Moreover, the antibiotic decomposition in the presence of humic acid, NaNO3, NaCl and Na2SO4 were conducted. The influence of reaction solution pH was studied by adjusting the initial pH of the solution (3.3 ∼ 11.7) with HCl or NaOH solution. The chemical stability of the photocatalyst was also investigated by reusing seven times in the catalytic system. After each cycle, the fresh dye with desired concentration was spiked into the solution for further run. Moreover, the photo-sulfate based oxidant driven reaction was examined for three dye molecules (indigo carmine, sunset yellow and tartrazine). Herein, all batch studies were carried out as triplicate independent experiments and average results were reported. The antibiotic removal efficiency was calculated by following equation:
(1) Removal %= (C0-C)/C0 × 100 %
where C0 and C are the initial and final concentrations at 0 and t min of irradiation time, respectively.

In addition, the intermediates of CPX degradation were analyzed by gas chromatography coupled to the mass spectrometer (GC-MS). The experimental temperature was kept at 50 °C for 4 min and reached 300 °C for 10 min with a rate of 8 °C min−1. Moreover, the detector was performed at 300 °C [22].

Box-Behnken Design (BBD) in response surface methodology was performed to assess the interaction between parameters towards the degradation of ciprofloxacin (Y). For this, three parameters were selected: catalyst dosage (g/L), PMS concentration (mM) and initial antibiotic concentration (mg/L). Design-Expert software was used for generating and evaluating the statistical experimental design. Total of 17 experiments were designed and they were conducted according to a three-level scale, i.e., low (−1), central (0) and high (+1). Table 1 shows the levels of independent variables. According to the BBD data process, these factors were evaluated by a second-order polynomial with the three factors (Eq. 2) [23]:
(2)

Where Y is a response variable (% antibiotic degradation), β represents a set of regression coefficients (also called constants), k is the number of exogenous variables (independent variables), and Ɛ an unknown constant error vector. In this equation, the positive sign is attributed the synergistic effect between parameters, while the negative sign represents an antagonistic effect. The optimal values of the parameters were obtained through the estimation of the regression equation and the interpretation of the response of the contour plots.

The powder XRD patterns of bare CeFeO3 and CeFeO3@NCQDs catalysts are shown in Fig. 2. For the pristine CeFeO3, the characteristic Bragg reflection peaks were detected at 2θ = 33.1◦, 35.6◦, 40.8◦, 47.8◦, 49.4◦, 54.2◦, 56.7◦, 62.5◦, 77.8◦ which could be indexed to (1 1 2), (0 2 1), (2 0 2), (0 0 4), (2 2 1), (3 1 1), (0 2 4), (2 2 4) and (1 1 6) planes of orthorhombic CeFeO3 (JCPDS Card No. 22–0166). In addition, two peaks were observed at 2θ = 64.3◦ and 70.1◦, corresponding to the CeO2 phase as impurity in the structure. The XRD pattern of CeFeO3@NCQDs composite was found similar to that of bare CeFeO3, demonstrating high dispersion of NCQDs over the surface. It could be also stated that no disruption to crystal structure of CeFeO3 occurred after incorporating with NCQDs. It is worth to mention that the characteristic peaks of CeFeO3 were more intense in the pattern of CeFeO3@NCQDs and the peaks shifted lower angle which could be ascribed to the internal stresses in lattice parameters caused by introduction of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots [24]. It should be also noted that a small characteristic peak of NCQDs was detected near 24.2◦, reflecting the presence of NCQDs [25]. The average crystallite sizes for bare CeFeO3 and CeFeO3@NCQDs catalysts were determined by using the Debye-Scherrer equation (Eq.3):
(3)
D =
0.9 λ
β Cos θ
where D is crystallite size, λ is the wavelength of the incident X-ray (1.54 Å), β is full width at half maxima (FWHM), and θ is diffraction angle corresponding to the most intense peak ( (1 1 2) crystal plane for CeFeO3). The crystal size of CeFeO3 was found as 14.5 nm which was two times higher than the crystallite size of CeFeO3@NCQDs (7.79 nm). This is could be attributed to the heterostructure induced lattice strain phenomenon as observed by similar studies related with NCQDs [26–28]. These results revealed that incorporation of NCQDs in CeFeO3 did not alter the unit cell crystal identity of CeFeO3, but changed the crystallite size more smaller than bare CeFeO3.

SEM and TEM techniques were used to examine the morphological structure of the as-synthesized catalysts. Fig. 3 shows the SEM images of the materials. The raw CeFeO3 perovskite exhibited mesoporous grain/sponge like structure which is consistent with literature [29]. In case of CeFeO3 @NCQDs, some agglomeration was observed which might have occurred during incorporation of NCQDs into the structure CeFeO3. It is also known that the hybridization with NCQDs provides formation of larger spatial networks in macroscopic level [30]. Based on the EDX analysis for the bare CeFeO3, the atomic percentages of cerium, iron and oxygen were obtained as 12.0 % 15.4 % and 72.5 %, respectively; while nitrogen (4.03 at%) and carbon (15.6 at%) atoms were detected in the spectrum of CeFeO3 @NCQDs. The corresponding element mapping images showed the homogenous distribution of perovskite elements (Ce, Fe and O) in both CeFeO3 and CeFeO3 @NCQDs samples while the maps also demonstrated the uniform dispersion of nitrogen and carbon atoms in the CeFeO3 @NCQDs structure. It can be deduced form the morphological analyses that the CeFeO3 @NCQDs catalyst was successfully synthesized through mixing method.

The microstructure of CeFeO3 and CeFeO3 @NCQDs samples further characterized by TEM analysis ( Fig. 4). The TEM image of bare CeFeO3 catalyst revealed spherical morphology with an average particle size of 14–15 nm which was in good agreement with the value obtained by Debye-Scherrer’s equation. In case of CeFeO3 @NCQDs sample, the uniform distribution of quantum dots with diameters ranging from 4 to 8 nm was observed on the surface of CeFeO3, further illustrating the successful doping of NCQDs into the structure.

Fig. 5 presents the Raman spectra of bare CeFeO3 and CeFeO3 @NCQDs. Considering the results of XRD analysis which orthorhombic crystal structure with Pbnm space group was observed for CeFeO3 perovskite, the vibration modes of Raman might be indexed at Brillouin zone centre as [31]:
(4) Г = 7Ag + 7B1 g + 5B2 g + 5B3 g + 8Au + 8B1 u + 10B2 u + 10B3 u

It is known that 24 vibration modes are Raman active in this group. In the spectrum of CeFeO3, seven Raman active peaks at around 112, 190, 275, 321, 440, 507 and 614 cm–1 were observed corresponding to the Ce–O (Ag), Ce–O (B3 g), FeO6 (Ag), FeO6 (Ag), Ce–O (Ag), Fe–O (Ag), Fe–O (B1 g), respectively [31]. The modes between 200 and 400 cm–1 could be ascribed to the tilt angles of the FeO6 octahedron in CeFeO3 while the modes at 400–500 cm–1 were related with external oxygen bending vibrations. The peaks above 500 cm–1 were attributed to the symmetric Fe–O stretching vibrations [31,32]. It was noteworthy that the Raman band at 440 cm–1 for Ce–O was more intense than other peaks related with Fe–O groups. It was speculated that weaker Fe-O peaks could be the sign of incorporation of Fe atoms into the CeFeO3 structure [33]. Manwar and co-workers [29] underlined the fact that the predominant band at about 440–450 cm–1 related with F2 g mode corresponded to the fluorite structure of ceria. In contrast to this argument, Gu and co-workers [34] emphasized that the higher intensity at 440 cm–1 could be related with the strong absorption feature of iron oxides at the wavenumber region which resulted weaker Raman bands than that of Ce–O. On the other hand, after the introduction of NCQDs into the CeFeO3 structure, the intensities of the characteristic Raman peaks significantly decreased possibly due to the coverage of surface by quantum dots, demonstrating the change in surface structure of CeFeO3 @NCQDs. Similar phenomena were observed by other researchers [28,35].

In order to further examine the chemical states and surface composition of bare CeFeO3 and CeFeO3 @NCQDs, XPS technique was conducted ( Fig. 6). The survey XPS spectra indicated that both CeFeO3 and CeFeO3 @NCQDs mainly consisted of Ce, Fe and O elements, while CeFeO3 @NCQDs displayed weak peaks of N and C. High resolved core level spectra of Ce 3d and Fe 2p verified that cerium and iron are in + 3 valence states, demonstrating the formation of CeFeO3 perovskite phase In the spectra of Ce 3d, two predominant zones at ∼888 and ∼900 eV, correlate with the characteristic binding energies of the spin–orbit splitting of Ce 3d5/2 and Ce 3d3/2 respective chemical states of Ce3+ [29]. Moreover, a peak at ∼916 eV corresponds to Ce4+ state (3d104 f0) of CeO2 crystal phase which was also detected in XRD pattern [31,36]. The XPS spectra of Fe 2p regions presented Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 respective chemical states of CeFeO3. Each of respective zones were divided into distinctive peaks as 711.5, 718.6 eV and 724.4, 734.6 eV as a result of the presence of Fe3+ tetrahedron, Fe3+ (satellite in Fe 2p3/2), Fe2+ and Fe3+ (satellite in Fe 2p1/2), respectively [7]. The formation of Fe2+ state in the CeFeO3 structure was investigated by Jabbarzare et al. [37] and they underlined that during synthesis of ferrite perovskite, the oxygen vacancies as donors released electrons which were absorbed by Fe3+ ions while Fe2+ ions were released. According to this theory, it can be stated that the oxygen vacancies were responsible for the presence of Fe2+ chemical state. It is also worth noticing that the core-levels of Ce 3d, Fe 2p and O 1 s for CeFeO3 @NCQDs catalyst downshifted to lower binding energies when compared with bare CeFeO3, suggesting strong interaction between NCQDs and CeFeO3 perovskite. Similar phenomena was reported by other researchers [38].

In C 1 s spectrum of CeFeO3, the peak for C 1 s component at binding energy of 285 eV is distinguishable and usually corresponds to carbon contributing derived by instrument during calibration. In case of CeFeO3 @NCQDs, the high resolution spectra of C 1 s indicated a new characteristic peak at 288.3 eV which corresponded to sp2-hybridized carbon within the triazine rings (N–CN) [39], revealing the presence of NCQDs over the CeFeO3 structure [40]. In addition, no other C 1 s peaks were observed in the spectrum of CeFeO3 @NCQDs, indicating that there is no interaction of carbon with ceria or iron and the interstitial position of orthorhombic CeFeO3 kept stable after addition of NCQDs. The peak at 398.8 in the N 1 s spectra which could be indexed to sp2-bonded N (C–N = C) in triazine rings, implying that nitrogen atom incorporated with carbon element inside the carbon quantum dots [40]. Otherwise, Chen and co-workers [27] stated that the peak at ∼399 eV might be ascribed to the presence of graphitic nitrogen in the CQDs. As depicted in Fig. 7, for bare CeFeO3 catalyst, the high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s was deconvoluted into three peaks located at 529.3 eV, 530.7 eV and 532.3 eV corresponding to O2– of Ce–O and Fe–O present in the CeFeO3 lattice (Olatt), O2– of oxygen defects/vacancies (Ovac) and surface adsorbed OH/H2O (Oads), respectively [41,42]. In case of CeFeO3 @NCQDs sample, the deconvolution of O 1 s spectra significantly changed in which two main peaks at 529.0 eV and 531.1 eV were observed representing lattice oxygen (Ce–O and Fe–O) and C–OH/CO/O–CO bonds, respectively [39,43]. The observation of new oxygen group at 531.1 eV ensured that the as-synthesized catalyst possessed new oxygen-rich functional groups (e.g. carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups) which are beneficial in the photocatalytic reactions [31,44]. Thus, XPS results above verified that NCQDs were deposited on the surface of CeFeO3 via oxygen-containing groups without changing the original structure of CeFeO3 perovskite.

To further explore the optical properties of the as-prepared catalysts, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy was recorded to understand the efficiency of photocatalytic activity of CeFeO3 and CeFeO3 @NCQDs, depicted in Fig. 8(a). The bare CeFeO3 exhibited a significant adsorption ability under visible region with tail extending to 750 nm. The introduced NCQDs in the perovskite structure resulted a slight blue shift in the absorption edge, which could be assigned to the quantum confinement effect of NCQDs [45]. Of note, the CeFeO3 @NCQDs composites still exhibited strong visible light absorption with high potential for solar light utilization.

Fig. 8(b) shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the NCQDs and typical absorption peak in the UV region of ∼300–370 nm was observed regarding the amine/carbonyl functional groups which was ascribed to the transition of n-π * electrons of the oxygen containing functional groups around the wavelength of 300 nm [44,46]. In order to examine the semiconductor nature of the perovskite material before and after incorporation of NCQDs, band gaps (Eg) were calculated using absorption spectra by plotting electronic parameters for Tauc expression ((αhⱱ)1/2 versus hⱱ) based on Kubelka-Munk function. As shown in Fig. 8(c), the band gap energies were calculated by extrapolating the straight lines of the curves. The band gaps of CeFeO3, CeFeO3 @NCQDs-5, CeFeO3 @NCQDs-10, CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15 catalysts were calculated to be 1.75 eV, 1.78 eV, 1.82 eV and 1.8 eV, respectively.

The photoluminescent spectra of CeFeO3 @NCQDs composites with varying NCQDs amounts were obtained at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm (Fig. 8(d)). The relative stronger emission intensity suggests the recombination probability of photo-induced charge pairs. All composites displayed strong emission shoulder at around 350 nm, the increasing NCQDs ratio apparently decreased the PL intensity of the composites in which the CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15 sample exhibited weaker emission intensity when compared with CeFeO3 @NCQDs-5 and CeFeO3 @NCQDs-10. This could be attributed to the excellent electronic storage performance of NCQDs, where NCQDs act as the reservoir of electrons thereby boosting the separation of charge carriers and hindering the recombination issue [47]. The slower electron-hole pairs recombination can be also useful for the rate of Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycling leading to enhanced photocatalytic efficiency in PMS-activated Fenton system.

Fig. 9(a) illustrates the valence-band XPS spectrum of CeFeO3 and CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15 catalysts. The valence bands (VB) of the samples were obtained by linear extrapolation of the edge of the spectrum to the extended baseline and the VBs of CeFeO3 and CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15 were at 0.90 eV and 0.50 eV, respectively, indicating that the maximum energy potential blue-shifted after addition of quantum dots. The conduction band (CB) values of CeFeO3 and CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15 photocatalysts were calculated as –0.85 and −1.3 eV, respectively, resulting strong reducing ability through organic compounds (Fig. 9(b)). According to the band alignment, CQDs could act an electron reservoir to trap electrons emitted from CeFeO3 particles under visible light irradiation, then the electrons were transferred to water or react with adsorbed pollutants. This indicates that the limited opportunity for electrons to recombine with holes which enhanced the photocatalytic efficiency, and at the same time improved the effect of holes to oxidize pollutants. Similar phenomena was observed by Jiang et al. [48] and they found that the incorporation of N/S-CQDs into MIL-101(Fe) structure resulted in a negative shift in the CB potential which promoted the thermodynamic driving force of proton reduction as well as enhancing persulfate activation.

The specific surface area and pore volume of CeFeO3 and CeFeO3/NCQDs samples were determined by considering BET and BJH methods. The BET specific surface areas of CeFeO3 and CeFeO3/NCQDs were determined as 26.86 and 3.21 m2/g, respectively. Similarly, the pore volume decreased from 0.162 to 0.012 cm3/g when the NCQDs was incorporated into the perovskite structure. The significantly decrease in the surface area could be due to fact that the carbon quantum dots filled the pores of CeFeO3. Similar observation was presented in the previous studies [49,50].

The photocatalytic activities of the as-synthesized CeFeO3 @NCQDs heterostructures were assessed for both visible light (Vis) and Vis/PMS driven CPX degradation. As shown in Fig. 10 (a), negligible ciprofloxacin removal was noted without any photocatalyst in the presence of light irradiation and the pristine CeFeO3 exhibited only 32.0% antibiotic degradation. The incorporation of N-doped carbon quantum dots with the perovskite greatly enhanced the photocatalytic decomposition efficiency: the relevant rate was calculated as 39.8%, 41.0% and 42.1% for CeFeO3 @NCQDs-5, CeFeO3 @NCQDs-10 and CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15, respectively. The improvement could be associated with slower recombination rate of the photogenerated charge carries than that of the sole CeFeO3 catalyst. In further step, the removal experiments were performed by adding persulfate ions into the reaction solution. As displayed in Fig. 10(b), the decomposition rates of CeFeO3 @NCQDs/Vis/PMS systems were significantly higher than that of the performances in the absence of PMS, which was assigned to the excellent sulfate activation capacity of CeFeO3 @NCQDs catalyst towards CPX degradation. The related performances were calculated as 84.8%, 86.9% and 92.5% for CeFeO3 @NCQDs-5, CeFeO3 @NCQDs-10 and CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15, respectively.

The promising activity of CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15/Vis/PMS system to degrade ciprofloxacin could be explained by considering different mechanism. First mechanism was attributed to the positive effect of NCQDs on the separation ability of the photogenerated electrons and holes. In this point, due to its π-π conjugated structure, NCQDs could act as an electron acceptor and, then form the coordination bond of the ferrite sites in the perovskite that provided quickly transfer photogenerated electrons from CeFeO3 to the NCQDs, which enabled the efficient separation of charge pairs and hindered the recombination rate. The second mechanism could be related to the change of the band structure of the bare perovskite (Fig. 9): the position of the CB changed so that the reduction potential was more sufficient to generate superoxide radicals and the VB also changed to produce a smaller band gap. The third effect was assigned to the oxygen vacancies which improved after the addition of NCQDs. The oxygen vacancy on the catalyst surface could optimize the adsorption energy with the contaminant and it could promote the adsorption of H2O and PMS, enhancing the photocatalysis and the Fenton-like reaction [51]. Furthermore, due to the photosensitizing effect of NCQDs, the transferred electrons could conduct back to CB of the photocatalyst, increasing formation of superoxide radicals [52]. The final mechanism was assigned to the formation of new radicals in the presence of PMS. In Fe(III)/Fe(II) sites, the PMS was transformed to the SO4• and OH• and the photogenerated electrons directly reacted with PMS adsorbed on the surface of the CQDs to generate the radicals. The new oxygen species especially SO4• provided further oxidation of piperazine ring in the CPX structure [53]. In addition, the advantageous effect of PMS was characterized by monitoring the absorption peak of the CPX centered at 278 nm by the UV–vis spectrophotometer (Fig. 10(c)). In further experimental studies, CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15 as a photocatalyst was utilized due to its highest activity.

The CPX degradation efficiency under visible light in the presence of CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15 catalyst was compared with those of other photocatalysts reported in literature, and the results were listed in Table S1. Considering the catalyst amount, the perovskite/carbon dots catalyst could be probably presented as the most feasible hybrid removal system.

A three factor Box-Behnken design consisting of 17 experimental runs was adopted to optimize experimental data. Table 2 summarizes the experimental design matrix that uses three factors as independent variables and one response as a dependent variable. An empirical relationship that relates the response and selected variables were obtained:

As indicated in the equation, the effects of catalyst dosage (x1), and PMS concentration (x2) and initial antibiotic concentration (x3) on removal rate constant of CPX (Y: response) were positive.

The quality of the models was statistically evaluated based on the coefficient of determination (R2) and the analysis of variance (ANOVA). The relevant results are shown in Table 3. The ANOVA of the quadratic regressions showed that the model was highly significant because the F-value (130.24) was greater than 0.001. The p value of the model was < 0.0001, meaning that there were only 0.01 % of the total variation that could not be explained by the model because of noise. Furthermore, the lack of fit F-value (15.39) had not statistically significant as the p value (0.0116) was > 0.05. Thus, it could be stated that the quadratic model could be successfully used for the prediction of the removal efficiency. According to the regression analysis, the quadratic model was also suggested to approximate the relation of independent values and experimental data. In the model, the predicted decomposition efficiencies were found in very close to the observed values, resulting a coefficient of R2 = 0.9941 and the adjusted R2 (0.9864) which is a corrected goodness-of-fit also approximated the R2 value. Fig. S1, which represents the experimental responses versus the predicted ones, demonstrates the suitability of the model. On the other hand, ANOVA results shows the important parameter and the interaction effects of the factors that influence the antibiotic removal performance. As presented in Table 3, the initial antibiotic concentration was the most effective parameter which was attributed to 582.43 F-value, also catalyst dosage (119.35) and PMS amount (60.78) followed the factor in order. Also, their effects were observed as < 0.0001 of p values. In addition, the mutual effects of the catalyst dosage between both PMS concentration and initial antibiotic concentration were statistically important, resulting from the lower p values than 0.05, while the mutual effect of the PMS concentration and initial antibiotic concentration showed insignificant behavior.

To investigate the interaction between the independent variables, three-dimensional (3D) response surface plots are depicted in Fig. S2. The catalyst dosage changed from 0.4 to 0.8 g/L, PMS concentration was in a range from 1.0 to 5.0 mM and initial antibiotic concentration from 5 to 15 mg/L The removal efficiency increased with increasing catalyst amount and PMS concentration. Also, it was observed that the photocatalyst dosage was more effective on the reaction, promoting the formation of the radicals. As the source of the sulfate radicals, a certain PMS concentration significantly enhanced the formation of the effective species, improving the oxidation of CPX molecules. However, at higher values than the certain amount the degradation slightly affected, since SO4• showed scavenging effect by excess PMS [54]. When the effects of catalyst dosage and initial antibiotic concentration were simultaneously analyzed, the utilization the CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15 improved with increasing photocatalyst dosage and decreasing CPX amount. In case of the lowest antibiotic concentration (5 mg/L), the advantageous behavior of the photocatalyst dosage was clearly observed, which could be ascribed to enhanced light excitation on the catalyst surface. When increasing organic molecules, the surface of the catalyst could be covered, resulting the penetration of visible light. In addition, the selective removal increased with increasing PMS concentration, whereas decreasing the concentration of the pollutant.

According to the model prediction, the maximum decomposition efficiency was 98.6 % under the optimum catalyst dosage (0.80 g/L), PMS concentration (4.90 mM) and initial CPX concentration (7.00 mg/L). The desirability of the model was 1.000. Then, to confirm the obtained optimized values from the RSM, experiments were carried out under the optimal condition. The removal performance of 97.8 % were observed, confirming the accuracy and reliability of the proposed model for predicting the CPX removal efficiency.

To gain insights into reactive oxygen species involved in the CPX degradation with CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15/Vis/PMS system, the catalytic reaction was carried out in the presence of various quenching agents. The photocatalytic decomposition performance of CPX in the presence of EDTA-2Na, BQ, IPA and EtOH was depicted in Fig. 11(a) and the reactions between the scavengers and free radicals were summarized Table 4.

It was observed that there was 52.4 % inhibition on the Vis/PMS-driven decomposition reaction in the presence of BQ. BQ could rapidly react with super oxide radicals with the reaction rate constant of 0.9–1.0 × 109 L/mol s [55]. Furthermore, the quinone molecules could activate PMS due to their carbonyl groups, resulting the formation of sulfate radicals which was effective on the degradation of CPX [56]. In other words, the decrease in the catalytic performance was attributed to the efficiency of •O2 and SO4•. To only observe the effect of •O2, the experiment was carried out in the absence of PMS and it was observed that the degradation efficiency decreased by 40.3 %, presenting the contribution percentage of super oxide radicals. Thus, the contribution percentage of SO4• in the degradation rate was calculated as 12.1 % by subtracting the percentage of•O2 (40.3 %) from the total percentage (52.4 %).

In case of EDTA-2Na, the photogenerated holes tended to undergo the oxidation of the trapping compound instead of CPX molecules, resulting that the reaction efficiency decreased to 61.1%. In other words, the contribution percentage of h+ was presented as 31.4 %. Notably, the EDTA-2Na molecules could oxidize into glyoxylic acid and ethylenediamine N,N′-diacetic acid, showing the low inhibition rate in the presence of BQ [57].

In the presence of isopropanol and ethanol, the activity slightly decreased by 30.8 % and 30.5 %, respectively. The ethanol which contains α-hydrogen can quench both SO4• and OH• with rate constant of 1.6–7.7 × 107 M−1 s−1 and 1.2–2.8 × 109 M−1 s−1, respectively. The isopropanol can be also used as a scavenger for the sulfate and hydroxyl radicals with rate constants of 8.2 × 107 M−1 s−1 and 1.9 × 109 M−1s−1, respectively [58]. The observations indicated that both sulfate and hydroxyl radicals could be considered as effective active species for the reaction, also the SO4• was more active than that of OH•, resulted from higher degradation activity in the presence of IPA. In addition, the related experiments were performed under visible light illumination in the absence of PMS. The degradation efficiency was observed as 18.7 % and 18.1 % in case of IPA and EtOH, respectively. According to the results, it could be stated that the contribution percentage of OH• in the decomposition rate was calculated as approximately 18.4 %, which was attributed to the average value of the percentage observed in the presence of IPA and EtOH in the visible light driven reaction. Furthermore, the contribution percentage of SO4• in the removal system could be proposed as 12.2 % by using the mean of the values which were derived from the differences between the Vis/PMS-driven and Vis-driven efficiencies in the presence of BQ, IPA and EtOH.

In summary, the superoxide radicals were the dominant reactive oxygen species for visible light-PMS triggered ciprofloxacin removal process. The other effective radical on the reaction was the active holes, also the sulfate and hydroxyl radicals partly participated in the decomposition process. The total contribution of these ROS was calculated was 102.3 %, however the value was found in the acceptable error level. Therefore, the individual contribution percentage of the ROS could be presented as depicted in Table 4.

According to the results, the antibiotic degradation in CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15/Vis/PMS system was schematically represented in Fig. 12. Firstly, the bare perovskite could be excited by visible light irradiation to generate electrons and holes. Subsequently, the transfer of electrons was occurred from sole CeFeO3 to NCQDs (as an electron acceptor). The photogenerated electrons of NCQDs and the remained electrons on the CB of CeFeO3 produced abundantly reactive superoxide radicals, which played an important role on the CPX degradation. The interaction of PMS molecules and the photoinduced electrons of NCQDs generated sulfate and hydroxyl radicals, which promoted the open-ring reactions. Furthermore, the ferrous species in CeFeO3 reduced PMS molecules to SO4• and OH•. In addition, the photogenerated holes on the VB of CeFeO3 (0.9 eV) directly oxidized the antibiotics molecules since its potential is not enough to oxidize H2O/OH to OH• radical (2.4 eV). The relevant reactions were presented as follows:

To further examine the performance of CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15/Vis/PMS, the antibiotic decomposition was performed in different water media. As seen in Fig. 11(b), the activity was found as 80.3 %, 76.2 %, 73.9 % and 71.0 % in case of drinking, tap, lake and sea water, respectively. That is to say, all decomposition performances were lower than that of distilled water (92.5 %). To estimate the differences in the experiments, the decomposition reaction was also conducted in the presence humic acid, NO3 -, Cl- and SO4 2- ions. As depicted in Fig. 11(c), the cipro removal efficiency presented a slight decrease in the presence of NO3 -, indicating the monovalent anion had negligible effects on the antibiotic degradation. The decomposition rate obviously decreased in the presence of anions. This could be due to the competitive adsorption between inorganic impurities and the functional groups of CPX on the catalyst surface. After adding anions especially SO4 2- and Cl-, the adsorption of the ciprofloxacin molecules decreased due to the increased ion strength and charge barrier [59]. The statement was supported with the result that the degradation performance was found in lower value in tap water than that of drinking water. In addition, in presence of organic matter the performance was markedly limited (Fig. 11(c)), observing the activities obtained in the lake water and sea water. three aspects could be important for the limited decomposition rate: the organic molecules could be bonded to the ciprofloxacin, preventing the migration of the antibiotic molecules to the catalyst surface. Moreover, the substance with large molecular size inhibited light attenuation, reducing the number of photons on the catalyst surface. In addition, the oxygen-included radicals could be captured by the organic pollutants [60,61]. Noteworthily, the coexisting ions and organics had small impacts on the CPX removal, indicating high selectivity of the CeFeO3 @NCQDs/Vis/PMS system.

The effect of the initial solution pH in the CeFeO3 @NCQDs/Vis/PMS system was studied and the related results were presented in Fig. 11(d). The highest removal efficiency of CPX was observed under neutral conditions (pH 6.2), while the degradation efficiency decreased from 92.5 % to 65.1 % with the pH decreased from 6.2 to 3.3 and with the pH value of the solution increased to 11.7, the CPX removal rate underwent an obvious deterioration to 36.1 %. The value of initial solution pH might affect the degradation of CPX by CeFeO3 @NCQDs/Vis/PMS process from several aspects. CPX was amphoteric compound with three pKa (3.64, 5.05, 5.86–6.35, 8.24–8.95) and presented as protonated forms (CPX3+, CPX2+ and CPX+), neutral form (CPX0) and deprotonated form (CPX−) species under different pH values [62]. When the initial pH was 6.2, the PMS molecules were efficiently activated via light irradiation and the neutral form of the antibiotic molecule could easily absorb photons, resulting in high production rate of the sulfate and hydroxyl radicals and sufficient ability for generating photoinduced species [53]. In case of acidic condition, the inhibitory effect could be due to the fact that free hydrogen ions bonded via O-O groups of the persulfate molecules, decreasing the incorporation ability between PMS and the catalyst [63]. Furthermore, HSO5 - could maintain stable, inhibiting the formation of active species and the bonding between the protonated species and the positively charged catalyst could limit the reaction rate. At alkaline condition, it could be stated that the amount of negative charge on the surface of the catalyst increased sharply, and the electrostatic repulsion between photocatalyst and pollutant was not beneficial for adsorption and oxidation reaction. Moreover, the ferrous species tended to form sedimentation, decreasing the activation of Fe ions via PMS [64]. This result showed that CeFeO3 @NCQDs/Vis/PMS could be greatly utilized in practical application under neutral condition.

The stability and reusability of photocatalyst are as essential parameters as its activity concerning industrial applications. On this basis, seven consecutive CPX degradation cycles were carried out under identical conditions. An insignificant reduction in the degradation performance of CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15 (15 %) was observed towards the seventh cycle ( Fig. 13). Furthermore, the stability was checked by performing XRD analysis for the catalyst spent in seven-cycle experiment. As observed in Fig. 13, the main characteristic peaks which was attributed to CeFeO3 were detected in the pattern of used photocatalyst. Only a difference was observed in the intensity of the peaks, which further confirmed the durability of the composite catalyst. Therefore, the relevant decrease could be due to fact that the antibiotics molecules covered the active surface area, decreasing the active surface area and the light-transmitting property of the photocatalyst. Furthermore, in the SEM image of the spent photocatalyst (Fig. 13) insignificant agglomeration was observed, conforming stable morphology in the removal system. The results indicated the acceptable stability and good reusability of the CQDs-modified CeFeO3 photocatalyst for practical water treatment processes.

To determine the possible intermediates of CPX generated during the reaction, GC-MS was utilized to analyze the resultant solution. The obtained information of intermediates was displayed in Table 5 and the two possible degradation pathways were proposed in Fig. 14. In the Route 1, piperazine structure firstly underwent ring opening reaction and the aldehyde derivative with m/z of 355 (P1) was generated. Then, the loss of formaldehyde group, decarboxylation and further the loss of nitrogen and formaldehyde groups were occurred in order to form product P2 (m/z = 267). Through the reaction pathway, the molecular structure of CPX was degraded, but the primary oxo-quinolinecarboxylic acid structure stayed remarkably stable. The mechanism of the piperazine ring stepwise opening was also observed in previous studies which examined the CPX degradation pathway in the presence of persulfate [65,66]. Thus, it could be stated that SO4• attracted the piperazine ring of CIP molecule in CeFeO3 @NCQDs/Vis/PMS system. In the Route 2, the CPX molecule was firstly transformed into by-product with m/z 281 (P3) via the cleavage of piperazine ring followed by defluorination. Afterwards, via further oxidation, the P3 intermediate was degraded into the derivative with m/z 265 (P4). At final stage, the intermediates could be further decomposed into short chain organic acids such as formic, oxamic and oxalic acids [67].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CeFeO3/N-CDs photocatalyst in dye degradation process
ce:section.ce:para: The activity of the photo-assisted CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15/PMS system was also employed in indigo carmine (IC), sunset yellow (SY) and tartrazine (TZ) decomposition under visible light illumination. As depicted in Fig. S3, the photo degradation rates were found high values of 99.2 %, 94.0 % and 82.1 % in case of IC, SY and TZ, respectively. One of possible reasons for superior IC degradation could be due to easily cleavage of CC bond by the sulfate radicals, which was attributed to self-indigo carmine degradation in the presence of persulfate. The other reason might be high affinity of IC molecules with ferrite-phases, resulting strong adsorption [68]. In terms of SY removal, the breaking of N = N bonds and open-ring reaction were provided with PMS-activated CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15/Vis system. TZ degradation was driven by low activation energy between its sulphonate groups and oxygen-included species (sulfate and hydroxyl radicals) [69]. Additionally, the reaction constant values were found in good agreement with the relevant degradation efficiencies, and they were calculated as 0.0425, 0.0019 and 0.0010 min−1 for IC, SY and TZ removal, respectively by evaluating the kinetics of the reaction via the pseudo first-order model as follows:

-ln(C/C0)=kappt where C0 (mg/L) and C (mg/L) are the concentrations at the beginning of the process and at irradiation time of t (min), respectively, as well as kapp (min−1) is the apparent first-order rate constant. In conclusion, CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15/Vis/PMS system worked as a promising candidate for treatment of colored substances in aqueous environment.

In this study, CeFeO3 @NCQDs heterojunction catalysts were successfully synthesized, which exhibited superior photodegradation rate towards CPX molecule. The XRD, SEM-EDX, TEM, Raman, XPS and DRS measurements were applied to characterize the catalyst properties. The effect of NCQDs on the optical and morphological features was ascribed to a satisfactory energy band structure and increased oxygen vacancies, facilitating photogenerated carrier separation and enhanced oxidizing feature, respectively. In line with the results, 92.5 % of CPX was removed in CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15/Vis/PMS system. The enhanced degradation efficiency was mainly attributed to the dominant role of the superoxide radicals on the decomposition of CPX molecules. The reusability of catalyst kept well after four runs and the main characteristics peaks were still observed in XRD graph. Furthermore, the performance of the heterostructure slightly decreased in the presence of ions and organics. The proposed degradation intermediates and pathways during CPX removal were presented in CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15/Vis/PMS system via GC-MS analysis. The CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15/PMS system was also employed in dye removal processes and the efficiencies were obtained as 99.2 %, 94.0 % and 82.1 % for IC, SY and TZ degradation, respectively. In addition, a three factor Box–Behnken experimental design in response surface methodology was applied to optimize reaction conditions. The catalytic removal remarkably decreased with increasing initial CPX concentration, while it increased with higher catalyst dosage and PMS amount. In the optimization study, it was observed that the maximum degradation rate (98.6 %) could be obtained under 0.80 g/L of catalyst dosage, 4.90 mM of PMS amount and 7.00 mg/L of initial CPX concentration. The capture experiments showed that the superoxide radicals were predominant active species for the decomposition of CPX in the Vis/PMS system. After 4th cycling experiments, CeFeO3 @NCQDs-15 heterojunction had good stability, leading its possible utilization in conventional applications. This work was expected to provide new insights for the design and application of CeFeO3 @NCQDs heterojunction photocatalyst to effectively degrade persistent pollutants in water.

The authors contributed equally in the following sections; 1) Evolution of overarching research aim. 2) Design of experimental studies. 3) Acquisition of data, or analysis and interpretation of data. 4) Drafting the article or revising it critically.
